items.title: How to make the most of your music practice time
items.editorNotes: Rewritten for clarity and readability while maintaining the original content's intent.
items.excerptBefore: Looking to optimize your music practice? Here are some killer tips, tricks, and tools to elevate your sessions.
items.excerptAfter: Learn how to maximize your music practice time with effective strategies, goal setting, and creative approaches to keep you motivated.
items.seoTitleBefore: How to make the most of your music practice time
items.seoTitleAfter: Maximize Your Music Practice Time
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Ever felt like your music practice time is slipping away faster than your favorite guitar riff? You're not alone! Here's the lowdown.
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Discover effective strategies to enhance your music practice sessions, including setting goals, taking breaks, and exploring different genres.
items.beforeBody:
Ever felt like your music practice time was slipping through your fingers faster than your favorite guitar riff? Trust us, we've all been there. 

Here's the lowdown on how to make every minute count:

1. Set the mood

Before we even start with the notes and scales, let’s talk environment. Your music practice setting can make or break your session!

  • Choose the right space: This could be anywhere from a cozy corner in your bedroom to that sunlit spot in your living room.
  • Eliminate distractions: Yep, this means muting those pesky notifications on your phone. Unless, of course, you’re using the Soundbrenner app for guidance!
  • Get comfy: Find a chair or cushion that supports your posture because, let’s face it, nobody wants a sore back from rocking out.

2. Goals, goals, goals!

It’s simple. Know what you want, and go get it! Aimlessly strumming away might be therapeutic, but it’s not necessarily productive.

Break it down:

  • Weekly targets: Maybe it’s mastering that tricky bridge section or getting that rhythm just right.
  • Daily focus: Dedicate each day to a specific music practice task, like technique on Mondays and improvisation on Tuesdays.

3. It's all about timing

Look, I love getting lost in the music as much as the next person. But let’s be honest, it’s super easy to lose track of time during music practice sessions. 

Get the Soundbrenner Core 2, a wearable metronome that vibes to your music. It also acts as a smartwatch, helping you stay on track. Pretty rad, right?

Take regular breaks

Ever heard of the Pomodoro Technique? It’s basically working in short, focused bursts with breaks in between. Science says it works, and who are we to argue with science? Apply this technique in your music practice sessions to improve your results.

Here's a simple routine:

  • 25 minutes of focused practice
  • 5-minute break (Stretch, hydrate, air guitar solos… you do you!)
  • Repeat!
Core 2 Core Steel

4. Mix it up

Repetition is essential, but monotony? Not so much. When it comes to music practice, a fresh approach can do wonders for your motivation and creativity!

Explore different genres

Venture outside your comfort zone. If you’re a classic rock aficionado, why not dip your toes in jazz, blues, or even flamenco? 

Playing around with various genres not only broadens your musical horizons. It also introduces you to different playing techniques and rhythms that can elevate your music practice game.

Swap your instrument

Been loyal to your guitar? Maybe give the keyboard or ukulele a shot. Learning a new instrument can be both fun and challenging, offering a fresh perspective on music. 

Besides, understanding multiple instruments can improve your music comprehension overall.

5. Reflect and review

Progress is rarely a straight path. It's a mix of highs, lows, and plateaus. The key is to be aware of where you stand and where you need to go.

Journal your journey

At the end of each music practice session, take a few moments to jot down your thoughts. What did you nail? What was challenging? 

Record your sessions

Thanks to smartphones, it's super easy to hit record and capture your practice. Listening back can be an eye-opener, highlighting areas that need more attention.

Seek feedback

Practice and share your music with friends, family, or fellow musicians. Fresh ears can offer invaluable insights and constructive criticism. Embrace it! 

Set monthly milestones

While daily goals are fantastic, it's also crucial to have a broader vision. At the start of each month, set a significant music practice milestone you'd like to achieve. It could be mastering a complete song, improvising a solo, or even performing in front of a small group. 

And finally, have fun!

The joy of music is in the journey, not just the destination. Enjoy every chord, every note, and every magical moment. The key to rocking your music practice is to stay mindful, focused, and inspired. With these tips, you'll be belting out tunes and dazzling listeners in no time!

If you would further like to streamline your music practice by setting practical and achievable milestones, then check out our blog for tips to set realistic music practice goals.

items.title: Top 10 must-watch music documentaries of all time
items.editorNotes: Revised for clarity and readability while maintaining factual accuracy and original links.
items.excerptBefore: Explore our list of the top 10 must-watch music documentaries that share the lives and stories behind your favorite artists and bands.
items.excerptAfter: Explore the top 10 must-watch music documentaries that offer a backstage pass to the lives, struggles, and triumphs of iconic artists.
items.seoTitleBefore: Top 10 must-watch music documentaries of all time
items.seoTitleAfter: Top 10 Must-Watch Music Documentaries of All Time
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Explore our list of the top 10 must-watch music documentaries that share the lives and stories behind your favorite artists and bands.
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Discover the most captivating music documentaries that showcase the lives and legacies of legendary artists. From Amy Winehouse to Whitney Houston, explore…
items.beforeBody:
Are you ready to take a melodious journey through some of the most captivating music documentaries ever made? From legends and pioneers to rising stars, these films offer a backstage pass to the lives, struggles, and triumphs of the artists who shape our soundtracks. 

Let's dive into our carefully selected list of the top 10 must-watch music documentaries of all time. 🎶🎙

1. Searching for Sugar Man (2012)

Source: IMDb

This mesmerizing music documentary follows the enigmatic search for Sixto Rodriguez, a forgotten folk artist who achieved accidental fame in South Africa. A true tale of music's power to inspire across continents.

2. Amy (2015) 

Source: IMDb

Step into the tumultuous life of the legendary Amy Winehouse. This music documentary provides an intimate and heartbreaking look at the gifted artist, her rise to fame, and the struggles that ultimately led to her tragic downfall.

3. The Last Waltz (1978) 

Source: The criterion collection

Directed by Martin Scorsese, this music documentary immortalizes the band's final concert in a star-studded farewell performance that features music icons like Bob Dylan, Eric Clapton, and Joni Mitchell.

4. 20 Feet from Stardom (2013) 

Source: IMDb

Uncover the untold stories of the backup singers who added depth and soul to some of the greatest hits in music history. This music documentary sheds light on the unsung heroes of the industry.

5. Woodstock (1970) 

Source: IMDb

Relive the iconic 1969 Woodstock Music Festival, a pivotal moment in music history. This music documentary shows their electrifying performances and candid behind-the-scenes moments that define a generation.

6. Kurt Cobain: Montage of Heck (2015) 

Source: IMDb

Delve into the mind of the enigmatic Kurt Cobain, the frontman of Nirvana. This music documentary offers an intimate portrait of his creative genius, struggles with fame, and his ultimate tragic end.

7. Gimme Shelter (1970) 

Source: The criterion collection

Journey with The Rolling Stones as they prepare for their infamous 1969 Altamont Free Concert. This music documentary captures the raw energy of the band and the turbulent times of the era.

8. What Happened, Miss Simone? (2015) 

Source: IMDb

This documentary paints a vivid picture of Nina Simone, an artist whose music and activism challenged social norms, leaving an unforgettable mark on both the music industry and the civil rights movement.

9. Don't Look Back (1967) 

Source: Filmaffinity

Join the legendary Bob Dylan during his groundbreaking 1965 tour of England. This music documentary offers an intimate look at his life and artistic evolution during a pivotal period in music history.

10. Whitney (2018) 

Source: The Movie Database

Explore the dramatic rise and tragic fall of Whitney Houston, one of the greatest voices in the history of music. It delves into her immense talent, personal struggles, and impact on pop culture.

Wrapping up

These music documentaries are more than just films. They are gateways into the lives, emotions, and legacies of the artists who have shaped the soundtracks of our lives.

If you enjoyed these amazing music documentaries, then we are sure you would also be interested in our curated list of the top 10 must-watch music films of all time.

Happy watching!

items.title: 10 fun facts about music that are surprising and rarely talked about
items.editorNotes: Rewritten for clarity and readability while preserving factual content.
items.excerptBefore: Discover 10 surprising, mid blowing and rarely talked about fun facts about music that will make you see music in a whole new light!
items.excerptAfter: Discover 10 surprising and rarely discussed fun facts about music that will change your perspective on this art form.
items.seoTitleBefore: 10 fun facts about music that are surprising and rarely talked about
items.seoTitleAfter: 10 Fun Facts About Music That Are Surprising
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Discover 10 surprising, mid blowing and rarely talked about fun facts about music that will make you see music in a whole new light!
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Explore 10 surprising and rarely talked about fun facts about music that will enhance your appreciation for this art form.
items.beforeBody:
Music is an ever-evolving world of art. There is always something new to learn and something new to know. If you are a music enthusiast who has the thirst to learn more, then you are in the right place! We've put together a list of 10 surprising and rarely talked about fun facts about music that will make you see music in a whole new light. 

So, let's get started! 🎶🎵🎼

1. The world's shortest song

Believe it or not, the world’s shortest song is just 1.316 seconds long! "You Suffer" by Napalm Death is the world's shortest song. Blink and you'll miss it!

2. Musical plants

Plants can actually respond to music! Several studies have shown that plants grow faster and healthier when exposed to music. So next time you're gardening, don't forget to play some tunes for your plants!

3. The most covered song

The Beatles' "Yesterday" is the most covered song in the history of recorded music. Over 2,200 artists have recorded their own versions of it! Talk about leaving a legacy!

4. The most expensive instrument

The Stradivarius violin is the most expensive musical instrument in the world. It was sold for $16 million in 2011. That's a lot of dough for a fiddle!

5. The world’s largest instrument

The largest playable instrument in the world is the Great Stalacpipe Organ, located in the Luray Caverns in Virginia, USA. It uses rubber mallets to tap on the cave's natural stalactites!

6. Music and exercise

Listening to music while exercising can actually improve your performance! A study has shown that listening to fast-paced music can help you exercise longer and harder. 

 

7. The World's longest concert

The world's longest concert is currently still ongoing! The concert, which takes place in Halberstadt, Germany, started in 2001 and is planned to end in 2640. That's a whopping 639 years long!

 

8. The first music ever recorded

The first music ever recorded was "Au Clair de la Lune" by Édouard-Léon Scott de Martinville in 1860. A phonautograph was used to record it. It is a device that can visually record sound waves.

9. The power of music on memory

Music can actually help improve your memory while studying! A study has shown that listening to music can help improve recall and memory performance. So next time you study, don't forget to put on some tunes!

10. The oldest known musical instrument

A flute made from a bird bone and mammoth ivory is the oldest known musical instrument in history. It was found in the Swabian Alps in Germany and is estimated to be over 35,000 years old!

Bottom line

Next time you're listening to your favorite tunes, remember these fun facts and appreciate the magic of music even more! Comment which fun fact was your favorite. If you would like to learn more facts about music, check out our blog on 10 interesting music traditions from around the world.

And don't forget, if you're a musician looking to improve your practice and performance, check out our range of products at Soundbrenner. Our wearable metronomes, the Soundbrenner Core and Pulse, can help you keep time and play with more precision. 

Happy playing!🎶

items.seoDescriptionAfter: Explore the must-have cello accessories and tools that every cellist, from beginners to professionals, should include in their toolkit for an enhanced…
items.beforeBody:
Whether you're a new cellist or a professional, you need to get yourself these accessories and tools! Your collection of cello accessories is a treasure chest of essentials that ensure your musical journey remains harmonious. Let's dive in!

1. Soundbrenner's wearable metronomes

Core 2 Core Steel Pulse

The Soundbrenner Core 2 and Pulse vibrating metronomes offer a revolutionary way to enhance your cello practice. Instead of the usual audio clicks, these devices allow you to feel the rhythm with vibrations seven times stronger than the average smartphone. The app lets you easily customize rhythmic elements such as BPM, time signature, and accents.

Whether you strap it to your wrist, arm, or ankle, the versatility of these metronomes ensures that you physically feel each beat. Additional features like precision tuning, dB meters, and smart notifications make these metronomes an essential accessory for every cellist.

2. Cello mute

Buy now

A cello mute is a useful accessory to control your instrument's volume, that allows for more nuanced dynamics and enable you to practice quietly when needed. Having a mute on hand gives you the flexibility to adapt to different performance settings, whether you're playing in a small chamber ensemble or a large orchestra, ensuring optimal sound quality.

Compact and lightweight, a cello mute takes up minimal space in your cello accessories kit, so it's always there when you need it, preventing last-minute scrambles before a performance or practice session.

3. Minuendo lossless earplugs

Buy now

Minuendo lossless earplugs are an essential accessory for cellists focused on auditory health without compromising sound quality. They are the world's first-ever variable passive Hi-Fi earplugs. It features patented membrane technology and micrometer precision craftsmanship, eliminating the frequency degradation common in foam earplugs.

A flat frequency response ensures natural, clear sound, placing them on par with custom-molded options. Notably, these earplugs offer stepless adjustments, allowing users to manually adjust noise reduction levels between -7dB and -25dB. With an extensive range of ear tips and a magnetic locking system, Minuendo earplugs are versatile and practical for any musical setting.

4. Endpin stop 

Buy now

An endpin stop is essential for any cellist, acting as a reliable anchor point for your cello during performances or practice sessions. It provides excellent stability, preventing unwanted slipping and movement. Made from durable materials, it easily attaches to various floor types, allowing you to focus solely on your music. Its compact size makes it a perfect addition to your cello accessories kit.

5. Stomp Bluetooth foot pedal

Buy now

Elevate your cello practice and performances with Stomp, the top-rated Bluetooth foot pedal that offers unmatched control and reliability. Imagine effortlessly scrolling through sheet music or managing your metronome with a mere foot tap. Built-in the US, Stomp's robust metal enclosure is designed to withstand any gigging environment.

With six customizable buttons that provide silent yet tactile feedback, this hands-free device ensures you'll never lose your place while performing. Long rehearsals? No worries—Stomp offers multiple power options, running for 150+ hours on a 9V battery or via a 9VDC adapter.

And it doesn't stop there; you can even charge your devices on the go with its built-in USB port. Its impressive 50-foot range ensures constant connectivity, and it’s compatible with any Bluetooth 4.0-enabled device. Make Stomp an indispensable part of your cello accessory toolkit.

6. Bow

Buy now

Your bow and cello are like two peas in a pod. Keep your bow securely in place, ideally in a dedicated bow holder within your cello case. A well-maintained bow is essential for producing those melodious notes.

7. Rosin

Buy now

Rosin is like magic dust. It is the secret behind a bow's grip on the strings. Make sure it's always there in your cello accessories kit, ready to rejuvenate your bow's magical powers.

8. Wolf eliminator 

Buy now

Wolf eliminators are an essential accessory for cellists seeking to refine their sound quality. These devices are easy to install and are designed to reduce wolf tones - those unwanted, howling sounds that can occur on certain notes.

By attaching a wolf eliminator to your cello's string, you enhance tonal clarity and achieve a more harmonious sound, letting you focus on your performance rather. It's extremely useful for any serious cellist.

9. Cleaning kit

Buy now

Your cello deserves some tender loving care. A cleaning kit, complete with a soft cloth and cello-friendly cleaning solution, helps keep your instrument looking and sounding its best.

10. Music stand

Buy now

A foldable music stand is an excellent addition to your collection of cello accessories. It allows you to practice wherever you please, no matter the venue.

Wrapping up

In conclusion, your collection of cello accessories is a guardian of your musical dreams. These ten must-haves are the backbone of your musical toolkit, and they ensure that your cello, and you are always in perfect harmony.

If you are a beginner looking for tips on incorporating a metronome into your cello practices, don't miss our blog post, where cellist teacher Sarah Rogerson shares her tips and experiences with the Soundbrenner Pulse.

items.title: The importance of meter in music - practical applications
Creating the perfect home music studio for beginners
items.editorNotes: Rewritten for clarity and readability while maintaining original content and links.
items.excerptBefore: Looking to produce your own music in the comfort of your home? Here is our guide on how to create the perfect home music studio for beginners
items.excerptAfter: Learn how to create the perfect home music studio for beginners with essential tips on room selection, equipment, and acoustic treatment.
items.seoTitleBefore: Creating the perfect home music studio for beginners
items.seoTitleAfter: Creating the Perfect Home Music Studio for Beginners
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Looking to produce your own music at home? Here is our guide on how to create the perfect home music studio for beginners.
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Discover essential tips for setting up a home music studio, including room selection, necessary equipment, and acoustic treatment for optimal sound…
items.beforeBody:
A growing number of budding music artists, photographers, and videographers are deciding to set up their own home music studio, streaming, or photography studios. Because why not? For the cost of renting a professional space a few times, you could invest in your own studio that you can use anytime you’re feeling inspired. You can record music, podcast or YouTube channel videos. Or, you can shoot still photos, all conveniently in your own space. This is the perfect setup for many YouTube stars, and it won’t cost a fortune if you set it up carefully.

We have some helpful advice about how to choose a room and convert it into the home music studio of your dreams (within your budget) and a superb outlet for your creativity. 

Choosing the perfect room

First, you’ll need to decide which room in your house will make the best home music studio location. 

These are some important factors:

  • Size and dimensions of the room,
  • Its surfaces (such as walls, ceiling, and flooring)
  • The ceiling height, and noise levels inside and outside the room

An ideal recording studio is large and provides plenty of room for musicians, instruments, recording equipment, and other gear.

Try to pick the quietest room

Along with size, consider a room that’s quiet—the quieter, the better. Go inside each room and listen for outside noises that could show up, uninvited, in your recordings. Barking dogs, children playing, a TV in the room next door?

Floor, ceiling, and walls

You don’t want a square room or a concrete floor, and you don’t want very low ceilings or drywall walls that are parallel. Floor-to-ceiling windows or large mirrors also are no-nos in a home music studio.

You can always add more sound-absorbing materials to hard walls and low ceilings.

Wood floors are a good choice for a recording studio room because carpeting can result in muffled recorded sounds.

300>

Internet connectivity

And if you are planning on streaming your own content, you might want to ensure your room is equipped with goodinternet connectivity! You can also find a lot of great resources for content creatorshere.

Home music studio essentials

There are some must-haves for your home music studio.

These include:

  • A good computer to record, mix, and store music, along with music-mixing software
  • An audio interface
  • A microphone with a pop filter
  • A microphone stand and cable
  • Headphones or monitor speakers
  • You’ll also want a desk, monitor speaker stands, and a comfortable recording chair.

USB audio interface

The USB audio interface lets you connect your headphones, microphone, and headphones to your computer. The more interface connections it contains, the more versatility you’ll have when recording.

You can start with a moderately-priced interface and then upgrade as needed. Keep in mind that you’ll need more channels for projects involving drums or a number of vocals at one time.

Check out our guide to home recording studio equipment for beginners

A good microphone

When choosing a microphone, buy a mid-range one with a large diaphragm condenser that’s good for vocals and acoustics like acoustic guitars. Don’t forget a microphone pop filter to minimize the “pop” sound that people make when singing or speaking sounds like “t” and “p”.

You’ll need one microphone stand per microphone. Invest in one that telescopes and adjusts well so you can place it at the perfect level for your voice, whether you’re standing or seated, and for any instruments.

Headphones

When purchasing headphones, remember that there are a couple of types: open-back headphones (for mixing) and closed-back headphones (for monitoring while you’re recording and mixing at times).

Acoustic Treatment

Acoustic treatments help make your space as “neutral” as possible, absorbing or soaking in the reverberation in your room when you’re recording and mixing.

There are three kinds of acoustic treatments: panels, diffusers, and bass traps. Panels absorb sound energy, diffusers scatter and spread sound waves around the room, and bass traps trap lower-frequency sounds.

Foam panels

Acoustic treatments, soundproofing, help your music sound more like your music and not like something recorded in your bedroom. There are numerous options for acoustic treatments in many budget ranges. You might consider foam panels because of their lower cost, but keep in mind they only absorb high-frequency sounds.

Because house rooms typically aren’t designed to become an audio production studio, you’ll need to do some trial and error before you get the acoustic renovations right. Take out all the furnishings, including any pictures or posters, mirrors, and furnishings that could vibrate, and then analyze the empty room by walking around and making lots of noise.

Listen, and gauge how much sound diffusion or absorption you already have and how much you may need.

Fibreglass panels

You can buy entire music studio acoustic treatment kits. However, to save money, you may want to make your own fiberglass panels. To make the panels more efficient, don’t forget to include an air gap between the panels and your walls.

If you’re on a very strict budget, instead of buying or making panels, you can absorb high-frequency sounds with objects around the house, including thick quilts or duvets, thick drapes, or even mattresses.

Don’t forget to add bass traps to room corners to help absorb lower-frequency acoustical energy. Bass traps help solve the “muddy” sound in your mixes so you hear the bass sound better.

Where to place your furniture

Believe it or not, where you put the furniture in your new music recording studio is important. Comfort and accessibility are important.

Do you have enough room to sit and play a guitar, bass, or drums, and also to stand up or sit down at the microphone when recording?

Does the space let you easily move from one spot to another? Is there room for others so you can collaborate or record a band?

Do you have room to mix your music after recording?

To help sound quality, remove from the room anything that can vibrate, including wall decorations or objects on your desk. Keep furnishings to a minimum.

Where to place your equipment

Position your monitors ideally at ear level, on either side of your computer, and carefully read the instructions that come with your monitor speakers. The placement of your monitors is extremely important and can make a big difference in how you hear the sound.

items.title: Dynamic range in music: The art of loudness and silence
Keeping time as a band: Why it isn't only the drummer's job...
items.editorNotes: Rewritten for clarity and readability while preserving original meaning and links.
items.excerptBefore: Keeping time as a band is one of the most fundamental things in performance. Here's why it's important, with some tips for improvement.
items.excerptAfter: Discover why keeping time as a band is a shared responsibility, not just the drummer's job. Learn how tight timing enhances both recording and live performances.
items.seoTitleAfter: Keeping Time as a Band: It's Not Just the Drummer's Job
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Keeping time as a band is one of the most fundamental things in performance. Here's why it's important, with some tips for improvement.
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Explore the importance of collective timing in a band. Learn how every member can contribute to a tight performance, both in the studio and live.
items.beforeBody:
Written by Chris Senner

Keeping time as a band is essential. Musicians have become too comfortable relying on their drummer to keep a performance tight. I believe that more emphasis can be put on the whole band or group gelling as one, as I believe this not only helps with recording but also performing live.

I've spent over 6 years touring the country, and one thing that I noticed was that almost every established band had every member playing to a click track. I was surprised by how well, so many bands play so tight together night in and night out.

Note: The Soundbrenner metronome is perfect for musicians looking to tour or play live shows. One of the coolest features that it gives you as a musician is the ability to play in-time off of a feel from a vibration, rather than a click track in your ear always.

Here are 5 reasons why keeping time as a band isn't only the drummer's job and how over-relying on your drummer can ultimately lead to poor performances.

Recording issues

Keeping time as a band is crucial for recording issues. When it comes time to start laying down your parts in the studio, being able to play in time will greatly reduce your producers' stress levels. This is true even if one of your band members is producing your album.

While you can quantize most parts, it's not always the best practice for all styles of music.

If you're relying on quantizing all of your parts in the studio, you may be becoming a little too reliant on technology. As soon as you have to perform live shows, you will have to perform your parts perfectly, only under a lot more pressure and anxiety.

My advice here is to do this the old-fashioned way. Before recording, be sure that you have spent time playing all of your parts to a metronome.

Your band's performance is only as tight as your weakest performer

While this sounds a little harsh, it is completely true. If you have 3 members absolutely crushing it, but you have a fourth player who is struggling to play in time, it will completely take away from your performance.

The solution to this is to make sure you are practicing together often. On top of this, it is also important what you are practicing. If you are just getting together to write music and not practicing running songs over and over, you won't grow as much overall.

Your drummer won't always be playing

Dynamics are often what makes a band so cool to see live. You will notice that drummers will occasionally drop out during songs, leaving the rest of their band to play on their own.

Rather than having the drummer lay down the kick drum or hi-hat, someone else should play to a metronome. This will ensure that your band stays tight.

I personally love it when drummers drop out and a piece of a song breaks down to just piano or guitar. This can lead to extremely effective songwriting and performances when the drums drop back in, especially if you're all on time.

Your skill is often judged by how well in time you play

The most important thing in performance aside from playing the correct parts and chords is timing. If you can't play in time the parts you've written in, then it doesn't matter how cool the part is.

The solution again is to practice your part of a metronome. While this is tedious, every great musician that you watch knows the importance of keeping time as a band.

When I began learning to play the piano, my instructor had me practice every song to a metronome as well as every scale or exercise.

I am forever thankful, and I believe this really led to confidence when it was time to play with others.

Acoustic performances

If you are playing a song acoustically, you won't always have your drummer. While you always want to practice with your bandmates to develop tightness, you also want to ensure you're also practicing by yourself.

Being prepared to play without your drummer allows you to perform your song the exact way you will want to.

Wrapping up

While there will always be times when you can rely on your incredible drummers, making sure your drummer has confidence that they can rely on you is also just as important.

If you're really wanting to improve your timing skills, you can do the following:

  • Practice scales to a metronome
  • Practice chord progression drills to a metronome
  • Practice the parts you write to a metronome
  • Practice other songs to a metronome

 

Chris Senner is the owner and operator of Keyboardkraze.com. He has spent the last 10 years writing music and touring the country with the band Vinyl Theatre. He now blogs about his experience in efforts to help other musicians.

Here are five reasons why keeping time as a band isn't solely the drummer's responsibility and how over-reliance on your drummer can lead to poor performances.

Dynamics often make a band exciting to watch live. Drummers sometimes drop out during songs, leaving the rest of the band to play independently.

I personally enjoy when drummers drop out, allowing a song to break down to just piano or guitar. This can lead to effective songwriting and performances when the drums return, especially if everyone is in sync.

Aside from playing the correct parts and chords, timing is crucial in performance. If you can't play your written parts in time, the quality of the part becomes irrelevant.

The solution is to practice your parts to a metronome. While this may seem tedious, every great musician understands the importance of keeping time as a band.

I am grateful for this, as it built my confidence when it was time to play with others.

While there will always be times to rely on your incredible drummers, ensuring they can depend on you is equally important.

  • Practice scales to a metronome

  • Practice the parts you write to a metronome
  • Practice other songs to a metronome

Chris Senner is the owner and operator of Keyboardkraze.com. He has spent the last 10 years writing music and touring the country with the band Vinyl Theatre. He now blogs about his experiences to help other musicians.

items.editorNotes: Revised for clarity and readability while maintaining factual accuracy.
items.excerptBefore: All the little habits count! Build a great foundation to your music practice habits through these 5 tips and practice them everyday.
items.excerptAfter: Discover five essential practice habits that every musician should develop to improve their skills and stay motivated.
items.seoTitleBefore: 5 practice habits musicians should develop right now
items.seoTitleAfter: 5 Essential Practice Habits for Musicians
items.seoDescriptionBefore: All the little habits count! Build a great foundation to your music practice habits through these 5 tips and practice them everyday.
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Learn five key practice habits that can help musicians improve their skills, stay motivated, and achieve their musical goals.
items.beforeBody:
Forming good music practice habits is a must if you want to stick to your goals and improve your musicianship over time. It’s easy for us to get distracted or lose motivation when we don’t have effective habits that keep us grounded in our practice routines. Here are 5 great music practice habits all musicians should be incorporating into their practice.

1. Stay consistent with set practice times each week

Most of us practice whenever we have a spare moment during the day. However, that is inconsistent and isn’t productive towards forming good practice habits.

For optimal results, set aside an hour or two each day or every other day to focus solely on improving at your instrument.

If you have a busy schedule, e.g. if you are a student or work long hours, try to at least make time for half an hour each day to develop consistent music practice habits.

2. Write your goals down

Many musicians have vague ideas for what they want to improve at, for example being able to play more scales or just playing faster in general. However, it is hard to achieve these goals when they aren’t defined properly.

It is a good habit to write down specific goals that you can work towards achieving, which will help you streamline your practice sessions and make it easy to see what to focus on.

How many scales do you want to learn?

Which scales are they exactly?

How fast do you want to be able to play them?

These are all questions you should be asking yourself as you decide on your music practice habits routine. Write these goals down on your phone or with a pen and paper. So that you can be constantly reminded of what you are supposed to be working on.

3. Record yourself playing

When we’re playing music, we often get lost in the experience instead of focusing intently on our technique or what our music might sound like to an audience. The music we play often sounds different to us than it does to our audience.

Because we already have an idea of what we are supposed to play, we might interpret our playing in a different manner since we already know what to expect.

However, to an audience, our dynamics may not be noticeable enough. Our tempo may be slightly fast or slow, or we might just not sound good at all. Recording yourself is a great music practice habit that can solve  this problem, by letting you revisit your playing to see whether it actually sounded like what you experienced in your head.

Remember to not just record the audio but also the video, so that you can identify problems in your technique and correct them.

4. Reward yourself

All work and no play aren’t very fun, especially for music. Consider implementing a reward system where you give yourself positive feedback for achieving specific music practice habits goals.

For example, you can allow yourself 5–10 minutes of “free playing” to play your favorite songs for each 30 minutes of serious practice.

This allows you to stay motivated while still achieving your musical goals. It’s important to keep having fun even when you’re practicing because music should not feel like a chore.

5. Find a practice buddy

Music is an inherently social activity. Try finding a friend who plays the same instrument as you, and set music practice goals for each other.

Besides fostering a little bit of friendly competition, it also keeps both of you accountable and allows you to have somebody to practice with.

 

These 5 practice habits are a great way to make sure you are continuously achieving your musical goals, staying motivated, and remaining consistent. So start incorporating these into your routine today and watch yourself improve!

If you enjoyed this article, you can check out our aritcle on "Tips for effective music practice sessions"

items.title: Top 5 songs to learn on drums for beginners
items.editorNotes: Revised for clarity and readability while maintaining factual accuracy.
items.excerptBefore: Are you looking for a new song to learn on drums? Here are our top 5 songs to learn on drums for beginners!
items.excerptAfter: Discover the top 5 rock songs perfect for beginners to learn on drums, enhancing your skills while having fun.
items.seoTitleAfter: Top 5 Songs to Learn on Drums for Beginners
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Are you looking for a new song to learn on drums? Here are our top 5 songs to learn on drums for beginners!
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Explore our top 5 rock songs that are ideal for beginners learning drums. Improve your skills with these classic tracks!
items.beforeBody:
If you’ve just bought your first drum set, you might be having a hard time deciding what to practice. Besides rudiments and basic grooves, learning songs is a great way to improve your skills as a beginner. Here are our top 5 picks of rock songs to learn on drums for beginners!

1. Back in Black (ACDC)

This classic rock song features a basic backbeat with some simple but effective fills. This is a great song to jam with, and will teach you the fundamentals of rock drumming-Back in Black

Want to learn fundamental of drumming from our community? Check out this article.

2. Seven Nation Army (The White Stripes)

Starting out with just a simple 4/4 kick pattern, Seven Nation Army is easy to learn on drums for beginners while also being incredibly fun to play. Just be sure to have a good pair of earplugs ready when things get loud.

3. We Will Rock You (Queen)

The easiest song on this list, We Will Rock You can be played using just the kick and snare drum, making it perfect for beginners who don’t want to waste any time before rocking out.

4. Boulevard of Broken Dreams (Green Day)

This classic Green Day song uses one of the most common rock beats. You’ll also have some exciting fills to play around with-Boulevard of Broken Dreams

5. Enter Sandman (Metallica)

Featuring an unorthodox tom pattern and unique cymbal accents, the most popular Metallica song is also the most challenging on this list. However, the skills learned by mastering this song will put you on your way to becoming a great rock drummer-Enter Sandman

As drummers, the number one skill you have to master is counting your beat, but sometimes we can't trust our rhythm which is why relying on a metronome is important.

items.title: How to really use your metronome: 9 creative ways
items.editorNotes: The article was rewritten for clarity and readability while maintaining the original content and links.
items.excerptBefore: There are more creative (and effective) ways to use the metronome, instead of having it hit every beat of the bar.
items.excerptAfter: Explore nine creative ways to use your metronome for rhythm practice, enhancing your timing and internalizing complex rhythms.
items.seoTitleBefore: How to really use your metronome: 9 creative ways
items.seoTitleAfter: 9 Creative Ways to Use Your Metronome
items.seoDescriptionBefore: There are more creative (and effective) ways to use the metronome, instead of having it hit every beat of the bar.
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Discover innovative techniques to use your metronome for rhythm practice, improving your timing and understanding of complex rhythms.
items.beforeBody:
Most of us probably use the metronome to hit every beat in the bar - to keep us in check. But, there are more creative ways to use the metronome, which develop your timing more effectively and help you internalize more complex rhythms. Here are some of our favorite ways to set up our metronome for rhythm practice:
(Pro tip - we save all these presets in a setlist, a feature available in our free Metronome App!)

1. Alternate bars on and off

Removing metronome beats will test your ability to stay on time independently.
Double the number of beats in your time signature (we have 8/4 here). Remove the beats in one half of the metronome cycle.

2. Displace the beat

Displacing the beat by one-sixteenth note.

Displacing the beat might take some getting used to at first, but change brings about growth, right? Feeling the accent in a different place will help lock in your sense of time.

We've manually put in the 16th note subdivisions here (so you have a visual prompt to keep you on track) by setting 16 beats in the bar (for 16 sixteenth notes in a 4/4 measure). Of course, you can also play around with displacing the beat by 2 or 3 sixteenth notes as well.

3. Son clave

How we like to set up the 3-2 son clave beat.

The 3-2 or 2-3 son clave rhythm is the most common pattern used in Cuban music and a great one to get in your bones.

We've set up a 3-2 clave beat here, with a small accent to keep the pulse on the 2-side of the cycle. Of course, you can customize the subdivisions as fits your needs. You can easily set up the 2-3 clave beat too by adjusting the accents within this 16-beat grid.

With the 16-beat grid here, we've essentially set up a two-measure cycle of eighth-note subdivisions (There are 16 eighth notes in two measures of 4/4).

4. 3:4 Polyrhythm

Can't quite figure out how 3 fits against 4? You can set up the composite rhythm in a 12-beat grid like so. Except for the first beat (where both patterns start), the 4-pattern is marked with the medium accent. The 3-pattern is marked with the smallest accent.

Use this grid as a refresher of how this polyrhythm sounds, or when you want to improvise over this.

For a more in-depth explanation: How to practice polyrhythms on the metronome

5. Beats 2 and 4

An oldie but a goodie. Set your metronome beats on 2 and 4 only to feel the backbeat - It's almost like you're playing with a live drummer.

6. Alternate between meters

Do you know when you're playing nice and steadily in simple meter, and suddenly a triplet comes the way that throws you off completely?

You can practice your fluidity between meters by alternating between duple and triple time: you can treat this 12-beat grid as a two-measure cycle (alternating between 6/8 and 3/4) or as a 12/8 grid with a strong 2-beat swing.

This is also a fun grid to improvise over!

7. Downbeat every two bars

Same concept as alternating bars on and off, but you can keep taking out beats until you only have a downbeat every two bars. This is hard!

8. Odd tuplets

Grouping odd tuplets or odd meters in 3s and 2s helps give more shape and phrasing to it all. In this example, we have a 3-2-2 grouping with accents.

Odd tuplets aren't the most common in most people's rhythmic vocabulary, but they can add some heat to your music and make it more interesting. Get used to counting in groups of 5s and 7s with the help of the metronome - with habit comes familiarity.

You can also set up a 10-beat or 14-beat grid if you want to play with two groups of 5s and 7s, respectively.

9. Ballad with no downbeat

You could set up a 6/8 ballad feel with the triplet subdivisions in our app, but we think it's way more fun to customize your subdivisions.

With this setup where you just get the off-beats, you'll have to provide that secure, locked-in downbeat, instead of relying on the metronome. Try it out with a bunch of 6/8 songs!

Check out: The difference between 6/8 and 3/4 - take the quiz!

Try out these metronome settings in the Metronome App by Soundbrenner! Don't forget to save your favorite settings as presets (setting up the clave rhythm from scratch is quite a pain - try it yourself if you don't believe me).

items.title: Famous jazz songs to play on piano: Our top 7!
items.editorNotes: Ensure the content is engaging and accessible for beginner piano players.
items.excerptBefore: Nothing beats jazz standards - they are easy to listen to which makes learning piano more fun. See famous jazz songs to play on the piano!
items.excerptAfter: Discover 7 famous jazz songs that are perfect for piano players, from classics like 'Take the A Train' to 'Fly Me to the Moon.'
items.seoTitleBefore: Famous jazz songs to play on piano: Our top 7!
items.seoTitleAfter: Famous Jazz Songs to Play on Piano: Our Top 7
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Nothing beats jazz standards - they are easy to listen to which makes learning piano more fun. See famous jazz songs to play on the piano!
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Explore our top 7 famous jazz songs to play on piano, featuring classics like 'Take the A Train' and 'Fly Me to the Moon.' Perfect for beginners and…
items.beforeBody:
Are you learning to play the piano? How about starting with some easy jazz standards? Here are 7 famous jazz songs to play on the piano.

1. Take the A train - Duke Ellington

This tune is hugely popular and considered a jazz standard written by Billy Strayhorn in the 60s. It was inspired by the new train service in New York and became the signature piece of the Ellington Orchestra. Mostly played in the key of C.

Famous songs “Exactly Like You”, “The Girl From Ipanema” and “So Danco Samba,” have the same chord changes if you transpose each into the key of C.

Make sure to create business cards beforehand!

Cm | Cm | Ab7 | Ab7 | Dm7b5 | G7alt | Cm | Cm

2. Isn’t she lovely - Stevie Wonder

A great jazz cover and an easy song to play for beginners. Stevie Wonder uses a chord progression that is usually used in Jazz. He also uses a lot of extension in the harmonies, which is what makes this song rich.

It’s in the key of E major.

C# minor 7 | F#7 | B7sus4 | E major 6

3. Moanin - Jazz Messengers

"Moanin'" was first recorded by Art Blakey's band the Jazz Messengers in 1958. It has been recorded numerous times and has become a jazz standard under a more specific genre called hard bop.

Check out this discussion by futurelearn.com which breaks down the song and explains what makes this a Jazz standard.


Bb | F minor | F major | Db | Ab | E | Gm | G

4. The way you look tonight - Jerome Kern

The way you look tonight was written in 1936 and became a classic jazz and pop vocal song. Made famous by Fred Astaire from the film Swing Time written  - which is another jazz standard. This song is the classic A-A-B-A which means verse 1, verse 2, bridge and verse 3.

5. Fly Me To The Moon - Bart Howard

Do we even have to convince you to try to play this song? We are sure you know this classic! Written by Bart Howard and made famous by singers like Frank Sinatra and Peggy Lee. With some versions having a more bossa nova tinge, while others have a swing tempo and an even 4/4 signature.


A | D7 | G7 | Cmaj7 | F | B7

6. Summertime from Porgy & Bess by George Gershwin

In the key of A minor, this composition by George Gershwin is usually performed with a distinctive medium swing/shuffle groove. Many of his compositions have become jazz standards, and people have made their own renditions of them.

7. My Funny Valentine

Cm | CmMaj7 | Cm7 | Cm6

items.title: Noise-cancelling headphones: Are they good enough for hearing protection?
items.editorNotes: The article was rewritten for clarity and readability while maintaining the original meaning and factual content.
items.excerptBefore: Noise cancelling headphones vs earplugs: which is better hearing protection? Noise-cancelling headphones protect you from dangerous noise
items.excerptAfter: Explore the effectiveness of noise-cancelling headphones for hearing protection. Understand the difference between harmful noise and unwanted sound, and discover safer alternatives for protecting your hearing.
items.seoTitleBefore: Noise-Cancelling Headphones: Are They Effective for Hearing Protection?
items.seoTitleAfter: Noise-cancelling headphones: Are they effective for hearing…
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Noise-cancelling headphones vs earplugs: which is better hearing protection? Noise-cancelling headphones protect you from dangerous noise.
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Learn about the limitations of noise-cancelling headphones in protecting against harmful noise. Discover certified hearing protection options and the…
items.beforeBody:
There is a common misconception that noise-cancelling headphones protect you from dangerous noise. While active noise cancelling (ANC) is very effective for reducing background noise like the sound of traffic, plane engines or air conditioning, and cafe chatter, it is far less effective at blocking other, more dangerous sounds.

The difference between harmful noise and unwanted sound

The "Noise" that noise-cancelling headphones remove is mostly unwanted sound.

For example, a loud conversation between colleagues in an open office environment could be a quiet noise that prohibits you from focusing on a task. The noise, many headphones advertise to cancel, is mostly static and periodic, like the droning of an airplane engine or the hum of an air-condition.

On the other hand, you have harmful noise. Loud and harmful sounds can be damaging to our hearing, even if it is desirable (like music). Your exposure to noise levels and amount of time is what makes a damaging sound. When exposure exceeds 85dB for 8 hours a day, you are at risk of acquiring Noise Induced Hearing Loss (NIHL). The time you can safely be exposed to louder sounds is much shorter. At 100dB, the level of a rock concert, the safety threshold is only 15 minutes.

Potentially damaging sound is not necessarily the same as unwanted sound. The problem is that people don't know if they are exposed to unwanted or damaging sounds. Therefore, many users that have noise-cancelling headphones use this as hearing protection, but it is often false protection!

The truth about noise-cancelling headphones

Most consumer-grade ANC headphones are not tested or certified as hearing protection.

On the other hand, CE-marked hearing protection has been through testing on humans to make sure that most people wearing it will achieve a certain level of protection, and have high standards for fitting instructions presented to the user.

The packaging is marked with a standardized reduction rating, typically an SNR and, or, NRR rating. The typical noise-cancelling earphones will not have gone through this testing, and the effectiveness is uncertain.

Noise-cancelling headphones reduce sound more than open or semi-closed headphones, and there is no same need to playback at high levels to drown out ambient noise. Still, most people don’t have the insight into what is actually damaging levels or how long it is safe to listen to music at a specific loudness.

The false sense of security may even motivate users to play louder music for long stretches of time, which will elevate the risk of NIHL.

I know that isolation means safety - wrong!

Imagine wearing over-the-ear headphones with a tight seal and an effective noise reduction. Yes, your hearing is protected from outside noise.

Even though they aren’t certified as hearing protection, they would probably function as such. You can be in real danger if you isolate yourself from your environment. There are cues in our sound environment that are essential for our spatial and situational awareness, like the sound of a vehicle approaching. Isolation by noise-cancelling technology may be even more disorienting, as they are disproportionately removing some sounds over others.

Many products are now implemented with hear-through functionality, amplifying the sound environment instead of reducing it. However, microphones, signal processing and loudspeakers have their limitations. The latency (timing), dynamic range (range of hearable levels) and frequency response (audible range from bass to treble) of the audio system don’t allow for an accurate representation of the sound environment. These limitations impede the user's ability to determine sound source direction and distance, and the result is an unnatural experience.

Noise-cancelling vs Industrial earplugs

Noise-cancelling technology is great for blocking out annoying noises like engines and ventilation. It is wonderful to be able to enjoy music without disturbances in noisy environments like on public transportation.

However, using earplugs certified as hearing protection is the recommended way to go if you want to protect yourself while:

  • Still being able to communicate
  • Being aware of your surroundings
  • Enjoy live music to its fullest

Looking for real hearing protection?

With advances in technology and design, many of today's earbuds now offer the ability to block out noise while maintaining a flat frequency response. One such product we have tried and tested is Minuendo.

Minuendo variable earplugs can decrease the level by 7 to 25dB with no frequency degradation. With a large range of ear tips to ensure a comfortable fit.

We have now partnered with Minuendo to offer this great product on our website. Visit https://www.soundbrenner.com/shop-minuendo to learn more!

The "noise" that noise-cancelling headphones remove is mostly unwanted sound.

For example, a loud conversation between colleagues in an open office can be distracting. The noise that many headphones advertise to cancel is mostly static and periodic, like the droning of an airplane engine or the hum of an air conditioner.

On the other hand, harmful noise can damage our hearing, even if it is desirable, like music. The risk arises from the noise level and duration of exposure. When exposure exceeds 85dB for 8 hours a day, you risk acquiring Noise Induced Hearing Loss (NIHL). At 100dB, the level of a rock concert, the safe exposure time is only 15 minutes.

Potentially damaging sound is not necessarily the same as unwanted sound. The problem is that many people do not know if they are exposed to unwanted or damaging sounds. Consequently, users of noise-cancelling headphones often mistakenly believe they are using them as hearing protection, which can be misleading.

The truth about noise-cancelling headphones

Most consumer-grade ANC headphones are not tested or certified as hearing protection.

In contrast, CE-marked hearing protection undergoes testing to ensure that most users achieve a certain level of protection, with high standards for fitting instructions.

The packaging of certified products is marked with a standardized reduction rating, typically an SNR or NRR rating. Typical noise-cancelling earphones do not have this certification, making their effectiveness uncertain.

Noise-cancelling headphones reduce sound more than open or semi-closed headphones, allowing for lower playback levels to drown out ambient noise. However, many users lack insight into what constitutes damaging levels or how long it is safe to listen to music at specific volumes.

This false sense of security may lead users to play louder music for extended periods, increasing the risk of NIHL.

I know that isolation means safety - wrong!

Wearing over-the-ear headphones with a tight seal may protect your hearing from outside noise. However, even if they function as hearing protection, isolating yourself from your environment can be dangerous.

Essential cues in our sound environment, like the sound of an approaching vehicle, are crucial for spatial and situational awareness. Noise-cancelling technology may disproportionately remove some sounds, leading to disorientation.

Many products now feature hear-through functionality, amplifying the sound environment instead of reducing it. However, limitations in microphones, signal processing, and loudspeakers can hinder accurate sound representation, affecting the user's ability to determine sound source direction and distance.

Noise-cancelling vs industrial earplugs

Noise-cancelling technology is excellent for blocking annoying noises like engines and ventilation. It allows you to enjoy music without disturbances in noisy environments like public transportation.

However, using earplugs certified as hearing protection is the recommended approach if you want to protect yourself while:

  • Still being able to communicate
  • Being aware of your surroundings
  • Enjoying live music to its fullest

Looking for real hearing protection?

With advances in technology and design, many modern earbuds can block out noise while maintaining a flat frequency response. One such product we have tested is Minuendo.

Minuendo variable earplugs can reduce sound levels by 7 to 25dB without frequency degradation, and they come with a variety of ear tips for a comfortable fit.

We have partnered with Minuendo to offer this excellent product on our website. Visit https://www.soundbrenner.com/shop-minuendo to learn more!

items.title: 5 tips for drummers to avoid rushing or dragging
items.editorNotes: The article was rewritten for clarity and readability while maintaining the original content and links.
items.excerptBefore: From practising with a metronome to separating your grooves and your fills, here are some tips to avoid rushing or dragging at your next gig!
items.excerptAfter: Discover five essential tips for drummers to avoid rushing or dragging during practice and performances. Improve your timing and rhythm with these effective strategies.
items.seoTitleBefore: 5 tips for drummers to avoid rushing or dragging
items.seoTitleAfter: 5 Tips for Drummers to Avoid Rushing or Dragging
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Here are tips to avoid rushing or dragging at your next gig, from metronome practice to separating grooves and fills!
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Learn how to improve your drumming timing with these five essential tips to avoid rushing or dragging. Practice effectively and enhance your rhythm skills.
items.beforeBody:
By now, musicians all over the world are familiar with this famous scene from Whiplash. It was one of the most quotable moments of the film and has spawned countless memes on social media. But as drummers, how do we avoid rushing or dragging ourselves? 

To answer this question, we must first understand the root cause behind the problem. Avoid rushing or dragging both can be caused by one common issue - practicing at specific tempos that feel comfortable to ourselves. Being creatures of habit, we all have preferred tempos that we fall back on when practicing grooves. For example, certain rock drummers might feel most comfortable playing at 4/4 100bpm, while jazz drummers may default to 120bpm in 6/8 time. This problem is compounded by the fact that most of us tend to neglect to practice with a metronome. Therefore, when the rock drummer is told to play along to a song that is at 80 bpm (again without a metronome), he will end up dragging. Likewise, the jazz drummer when asked to play a rock groove at 100bpm may end up rushing.

Practice with a metronome

 

The easiest long-term solution to this problem is playing along to a metronome or click track. Rushing tends to be a more common issue as opposed to dragging, so make sure to practice your grooves at a slower tempo, for example, 40 bpm. This will train your muscle memory and help you internalize the rhythm, and, counterintuitively, also will enable you to play faster in the long run.

If you're looking for a free solution, check out our award-winning metronome app.

Separate your grooves and your fills

 

We often insert fills at the end of a groove by default, and rightly so. However, it is important to separate the two during practice. Many drummers fall into the habit of knowing the groove better than the fill, and thus rushing to move their hands into position to execute the fill at the end of a bar or phrase. Knowing a fill front-to-back will enable you to play it regardless of where you insert it into the song, instead of only being used to playing it in conjunction with a groove. By practicing like this, you will not only build chops but also improve your timekeeping abilities and thus reduce rushing and dragging errors.

Record your practice sessions

 

When you are in the moment, it is easy to get lost in the music. While this isn’t a bad thing, you also become less fully aware of your playing when your body goes into autopilot mode.

This is why recording yourself can be so useful. By watching what you have just played, you can quickly identify timing inconsistencies and fix them during your next playthrough. To keep yourself accountable, you could also keep a video log to ensure you are constantly improving. Try uploading weekly practice videos and tagging us @soundbrenner to stay motivated.

Use a vibrating metronome

Speaking of Soundbrenner, our wearable devices are the perfect solution to your rushing/dragging issues. By feeling the beat instead of hearing it, you can more easily perceive the tempo of a song. Thus, play more accurately. If you are rushing, you will feel physical feedback as the vibration falls after you have played a note, and vice versa. This allows you to quickly correct tempo mistakes on the fly. Even better, the lack of a click track means you won’t be distracted by annoying noises.

Lock in with your bassist

 

If you are playing in a band, try focusing intently on what your bassist is playing. The two of you are responsible for holding down the rhythm section. Therefore, developing strong synergy with your bass player is a must. While it is a general rule of thumb that the bassist follows your rhythm, you should actively listen to each other and provide rhythmic feedback where necessary. Having a tight bass + drum combo is what makes good bands sound great.

And there you have it, 5 tips to avoid rushing or dragging. If you follow the steps listed above, you will never get a cymbal thrown at you during band practice (hopefully). Good luck, and happy practicing!

items.title: Songs for teaching rhythm to kids and young musicians
items.editorNotes: Revised for clarity and readability while maintaining factual content and links.
items.excerptBefore: There are loads of songs to teach rhythm - it makes learning fun. Here are our favorite age-appropriate songs with interesting rhythms.
items.excerptAfter: Explore popular songs that effectively teach rhythm to kids and young musicians, ranging from simple to complex rhythmic elements.
items.seoTitleBefore: Songs for teaching rhythm to kids and young musicians
items.seoTitleAfter: Songs for Teaching Rhythm to Kids and Young Musicians
items.seoDescriptionBefore: There are loads of songs to teach rhythm - it makes learning fun. Here are our favorite age-appropriate songs with interesting rhythms.
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Discover beloved songs that help teach rhythm to children and young musicians, featuring examples with varying complexity and engaging rhythmic elements.
items.beforeBody:
Are you teaching kids rhythm? Sometimes you want to teach with songs as rhythm examples - it's a more fun way to learn while giving context to the rhythms. Here are some beloved popular songs with interesting rhythmic elements (in increasing complexity), appropriate for all ages - check them out! (Playlist available below.)

1. ABC Song

Sing this with me: A-B-C-D-E-F-G...
And there you have your first half note, or your first rest, however, you want to teach it. It's such a natural hold or pause that it's a great way to demonstrate different note lengths.

Let's keep going: H-I-J-K-L-M-N-O-P
And there you've got an intro to subdivisions, or eighth notes, with "L-M-N-O-P."

And of course, throughout the song, these occur again.

Alternative example: "Frère Jacques", or "Brother John" for the English version. Similar rhythmic phrasing.

2. Row, Row, Row Your Boat

Since we're talking about subdivisions, "Row, Row, Row Your Boat" is great for demonstrating the triplet subdivision with "merrily, merrily, merrily, merrily," as well as compound meter.

We recommend encouraging young learners to move to this song too - the lilting rhythms are great for them to embody a different feel.

Did you know you can use movement to help identify time signatures?

3. Brahms Lullaby

A great piece to demonstrate triple time. The slow tempo also helps if you're connecting movement to the song - you'll just have to make sure no one falls asleep...

Alternative: "Edelweiss", The Sound of Music

4. My Favorite Things, The Sound of Music

Another one from the aforementioned musical, and a fast-paced alternative to demonstrate triple time. If your students are more advanced, the hemiola at "When the dog bites, when the bee stings" is well worth discussing too.

5. London Bridge is Falling Down

Use these songs to teach dotted rhythms. Though the dotted figure only comes up in the title phrase of the song, there's plenty of opportunity to create some rhythmic variations in the song. As an exercise, why not ask your students to add some dotted rhythms elsewhere in the song?

6. Un Poco Loco, Coco

A musically dynamic song from a beautiful movie - in 6/8 time with a sneaky hemiola in the beginning! For more advanced students, there's a lot to explore with the different rhythmic accents within the band and in the vocals.

7. Here Comes the Sun, The Beatles

This Beatles classic is rife with syncopation, and the occasional 4-over-3 polymetric drum hits between verses would be especially fun to unpack.

Enjoy using these songs to teach rhythm!

items.title: 5 essential drum beats for beginners
items.editorNotes: Revised for clarity and readability while maintaining factual content.
items.excerptBefore: Are you learning drum beats by yourself? Here are our top 5 drum beats for beginners. Pull them out anytime and anywhere.
items.excerptAfter: Discover five essential drum beats for beginners, complete with examples of popular songs that utilize these grooves.
items.seoTitleBefore: 5 essential drum beats for beginners
items.seoTitleAfter: 5 Essential Drum Beats for Beginners
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Are you learning drum beats by yourself? Here are our top 5 drum beats for beginners. Pull them out anytime and anywhere.
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Learn the top five drum beats every beginner should master, along with popular song examples to practice with.
items.beforeBody:
If you’re just starting out on the drums, it can be hard to know exactly what to practice - especially if you’re learning on your own. As the main timekeeper of the band, it is the drummer’s job to set the tempo, feel, and groove of each song. It is therefore vital that you have a solid repertoire of beats you can pull out of your toolkit at any time. Here are our top 5 drum beats for beginners, as well as example of popular songs that use the same or similar grooves:

1. Basic backbeat

The backbeat is the foundation of most modern drum grooves, and is often the first beat that most drummers learn. This simple but effective rhythm places the emphasis on beats 2 and 4 via the snare drum, while the kick drum only plays on beats 1 and 3.

This is one of the most common beats in popular music today, so be sure to practice it until you can play it effortlessly. 

Example: Michael Jackson - Billie Jean

2. Sixteenth-note backbeat

This beat is similar to the previous one, but utilizes sixteenth notes instead of eighth notes on the hi-hat. The most common way of playing this is to alternate between your right and left hands for the hi-hat notes (RLRL). You can also try playing the hi-hat with just your right (or dominant) hand to build your speed.

Example: Ozzy Osbourne - Crazy Train

3. Disco beat

Another modified backbeat, this essential drum beat for beginners adds an additional kick drum on the 2 and 4 along with the snare. Layer this beat with flourishes on the hi-hat or cymbals for additional texture.

Example: Daft Punk - Get Lucky

4. Half time beat

This beat is very useful when you have a song that needs space for the other instruments to shine (e.g. during a guitar solo), or to mark a section change without altering the bpm (during a bridge for example). Practice playing a simple backbeat in half time first, and then move on to more complicated rhythms.

Example: Rush - Tom Sawyer

5. Easy Shuffle

The shuffle groove can be a difficult one to master, so here’s a basic beginner’s version to get you started. This beat forms the basis of many iconic shuffle grooves such as the Purdie Shuffle, Rosanna Shuffle, and many more!

Example: Stevie Ray Vaughan - Cold Shot


For more drum content, check out our five tips for improving your metronome practice as a drummer.

items.title: The 100% Mindset: How to grow as a musician with every song!
items.editorNotes: The article has been rewritten for clarity and readability while preserving the original meaning and structure.
items.excerptBefore: Learning to play new songs can be a great way to become a better musician. This article explains how to grow as a musician with each song!
items.excerptAfter: Learn how to grow as a musician with every song you learn by adopting the 100% mindset. Discover the importance of tackling the awkward 10% and how to maximize your practice.
items.seoTitleBefore: The 100% Mindset: How to grow as a musician with every song!
items.seoTitleAfter: The 100% Mindset: Grow as a Musician with Every Song
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Learning to play new songs can be a great way to become a better musician. This article explains how to grow as a musician with each song!
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Explore how to enhance your musical skills with every song you learn by embracing the 100% mindset. Learn techniques to tackle challenges and improve your…
items.beforeBody:
Learning to play new songs can be a great way to become a better musician. But you need the right approach, or you may find yourself stuck on the same level. As many players have found, simply learning more songs is not a guarantee you’ll grow as a musician. In this article, I'll explain how to grow as a musician with each song you learn.

The Intermediate Plateau

When you first start playing an instrument, everything is hard. Everything feels a little unnatural and weird. Whether it’s holding a saxophone, sitting behind a piano, or fretting notes on a guitar. The fact that everything is a challenge as a beginner is great for your progress. Because everything is hard, it doesn’t really matter which song you learn or how you go about it. Whatever you do, it will involve things you have no idea how to do yet and through hard work, practice, and persistence, you’ll figure it out.

But things are different once you’ve gotten some basic skills down and have reached an intermediate level. More and more, you have the ability to learn songs without too much time and effort. On the one hand, that’s great: all the hard work you’ve put in earlier is paying off! There aren’t as many completely ‘new’ things you run into. But on the other hand, this is also the point that many guitarists stop getting better and hit a plateau.

The Awkward 10%

What often happens, is that you can learn 90% of a new song without any major problems, or major effort. The other 10% feels a little uncomfortable or awkward to play for some reason. So, savvy as you are, you figure out how to play that 10% in a way that sounds kind of similar but feels more comfortable. Maybe you use some different chord voicing that you’re more familiar with. Or you pick a strumming pattern that you’ve been playing forever, instead of learning the one in the recording. Or you change some things here and there to hide some of your… let’s call them imperfections.

I admit that I’ve used this strategy plenty of times. Especially when you have a gig coming up very soon, and you need to play something that does the job, this isn’t a bad strategy. But if your goal is to improve, ‘kinda learning a song’ is your enemy. You’re re-using the skills you already have instead of refining them or adding new ones to your skill set.

In other words, that awkward 10% is where improvement happens. It’s like what Muhammed Ali said: ‘I only start counting sit-ups when they start to hurt because those are the ones that count’. Similarly, if you can learn a song without too much effort, the gains will be limited. If it requires focus and intense practice (like when you started playing music), you will keep improving and grow as a musician. In other words, you should be thrilled about the hard 10%. That’s where real progress is made.

How to get to 100%?

When you adopt this mindset of always going for 100%, every song you learn becomes an opportunity to grow. That’s true for even the simplest of songs. While technology might be the obvious thing to focus on when you want to get to 100%, that’s not the only way you can make the most out of every song you learn. Here are three steps I’d recommend taking every time.

1. Figure out the song by ear

When you learn a song by ear instead of using TAB, sheet music, or a video lesson, you train two distinct skills. First, you’ll train your ears to hear in greater detail. You’ll practice picking out the different instruments, hearing the bass notes, dissecting the mix… This gives you a much deeper understanding of the song and everything that’s going on in it. Second, you’ll train your ability to translate sounds that you hear in your head to your instrument: to play by ear. This is a skill that helps you with almost any musical activity: from improvising to songwriting, and from rehearsing to performing on stage.

2. Analyse the music theory

2. Next, analyse the music theory underlying the song. A great place to start with this is to try and answer questions. Why do these chords sound good together? Why does this melody sound sad? By answering questions like these, you connect the sounds you’ve heard in the song to music theory. Connecting sound with theory is a great way to make sure your understanding of theory is both practical and intuitive. After all, music theory without music is just math!

3. Strive for Perfection

After a little practice, you can play the song, sure. But can you absolutely nail it? Can you play it flawlessly ten times in a row? There’s almost always something you can improve, whether it’s in terms of timing, feel, sound, dynamics… That might sound a little demotivating at first, but it certainly doesn’t have to be. Working to get areas of your playing from good enough to close to perfect is actually super rewarding. 

Practice slowly

Now, there’s one thing that’s critical if you want to improve your technique: practice slowly! When you are ‘polishing’ parts of your playing, you need to slow down to ‘unlearn’ what you’re used to doing and find a better way. I recommend using a metronome to make sure you’re not speeding up, and really taking the time to perfect your technique. Playing slowly has two advantages. First, it allows you to hear small imperfections that might be barely audible when you play faster. Secondly, you’re going slow enough that you can execute the movements perfectly. As they say, slow is smooth and smooth is fast.

The power of learning songs

Becoming a better musician by studying the music you love is great. Not only is it more inspiring than doing dry exercises, but it also ensures that you’re spending your practice time on the right things. For example, you could study sweep picking for hours and hours, but if you rarely need it in your playing, it might not be the best way to spend your time. By using the songs you love to play as practice material, you’re always focusing on the things that you need and will actually use in day-to-day playing. This approach is a powerful way to learn how to grow as a musician. Combined with the ‘100% mindset,’ learning songs can be a fun, musical, and super effective way to understand how to grow as a musician!

Just Rijna is the founder of StringKick, an online training academy for guitar players. StringKick is focused on helping you to train your ears, learn music theory and develop your musicality, all with the goal of making your musical journey more fun and fulfilling.
